reasoning: The Lean statement mirrors Rudin's Exercise 4.21(a) exactly: X is a MetricSpace, K and F are subsets, with hypotheses IsCompact K, IsClosed F, and Disjoint K F — matching "K compact, F closed, K and F disjoint". The conclusion ∃ δ > 0, ∀ p ∈ K, ∀ q ∈ F, dist p q > δ precisely captures "there exists δ > 0 such that d(p, q) > δ if p ∈ K, q ∈ F", including the strict inequality. No hypotheses are dropped or added, no types are silently changed (Set X, not Finset; MetricSpace, not pseudo-metric), and the quantifier structure matches. This is a strictly faithful formalization.
